title: System intent satisfaction: portability
scope: Governing intent: Keep package contracts portable across host repos, languages, agents, vendors, and local dogfooding environments.
goal: Assess whether portability is represented as contracts and boundaries rather than local dogfooding assumptions.
non_goals: Do not implement additional non-Python adapters.
Do not change local delegation target configuration.

review_mode.review question: Are portability claims backed by contract surfaces and local-boundary language?
review_mode.inputs inspected first: README, extension boundary docs, generated/reference docs, local override schema

review_method.commands used: rg portability/vendor/language/local/dogfooding/generated/type across README, docs, reference docs, and workspace docs

references.label: Extension boundary
references.locator: selective adoption and non-core module notes

findings.title: Satisfaction is medium: the contract posture is portable, but implementation proof is still uneven
findings.summary: The docs and schemas avoid hard-coding a single vendor/model and local config keeps delegation targets local. The strongest implementation path remains the Python/source-checkout workflow, so portability is better expressed than fully proven.
findings.evidence: Local override schema treats model/provider as opaque local metadata; capability docs are tool/vendor neutral; extension-boundary docs say first-party shapes are proven but non-core extension proof remains limited.
findings.risk if unchanged: Dogfooding convenience can become hidden product assumption, especially around Python CLI behavior, local uv invocation, and specific model capability labels.
findings.suggested action: Keep portability work tied to concrete adapter or clean-room evaluation lanes rather than broad doctrine.
findings.promotion target: adapter/evaluation roadmap only when a concrete portability lane is active
findings.promotion trigger: A non-Python adapter or non-Codex/Copilot/Gemini evaluation exposes a contract that is still Python- or vendor-shaped.

recommendation.defer: Use future adapter/evaluation lanes to test the portability claim.
recommendation.dismiss: Do not create generic portability work without a concrete target.

retention.trigger: superseded by a focused adapter portability review or implementation lane
retention.proof surface: adapter contracts, clean-room install proof, and local override schema

drift_log: 2026-05-08: Review created and filled from durable-intent doctrine pass.
